speaker: interviewer
text: We were just talking about your life in the ghetto. And you said that you sometimes went outside of the ghetto to get food. Did you find that your Polish neighbors were helpful to you? Were there any that rejected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not mostly the Polish. We-- I went close to the ghetto, people that lived close to the ghetto. And they was only Ukrainian. And they used to be our neighbors. We used to-- I used to bring him some tablecloth sometimes, and in exchange for food for potatoes, a piece of bread, and things like that. And then-- when I was caught, and I got a caught, they took everything away from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. We'll talk about that in a minute. Let's go back to the ghetto, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you when you left your home? When you left your home, and lived in the ghetto, what were you able to take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only clothes, whatever-- it was-- clothes, most of it, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you get the tablecloth to give your Ukrainian neighbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: We packed it up, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you snuck it anyway? Did you sneak out money?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I had no money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family, you don't remember if did any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the Ukrainians, the Poles, make fun of you, or call you names because you were Jewish? Did you find that they were mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, the other neighbor-- the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, or after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in the ghetto, we didn't see any Ukrainian people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you went to get food, when you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I hope that-- that-- that nobody saw me.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but the people that you went to, the ones you went to were nice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, they didn't make fun. No. The people were very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody that you went to for food that didn't give it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew-- I went to the people that we-- we knew. We didn't-- I didn't go to any other. Like for instance, my-- from my house, the-- the back of the house was already Ukrainian people that lived there. But it didn't happen too often, you know, it was just once in a blue moon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about other people in the ghetto? Did they do the same thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I assume so. But I cannot tell anything about other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only one in your family that did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most likely I think so, yeah. I was the-- the gutsy one, the risky one, [LAUGHS] too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother know you were doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you didn't tell her that you did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she did. She did I would say. I came home with the potatoes or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she didn't try to stop you?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you're hungry, nothing stops-- stops anybody. I mean, God forbid if you-- you should know from that. If you're hungry, you do what you can to be-- to get something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about the behavior of the people to each other in the ghetto? Were they sympathetic? Was each one trying to just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I can only tell you, with-- I-- I was with my-- I saw some of my friends. We were close. And I didn't think there was no-- about what kind of behavior there was in other parts-- houses or so. The house we-- we lived was fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: People tried to help each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were kind to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't like desperate, and just everybody getting for himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all had the same kind-- we all lived the same life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about if somebody got sick? Was there medical care available?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, one of-- one of my mother's relatives who lived in the house, she was-- she died in that house from starvation, or she was a little sick too. But together, she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a wall or a fence built around the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, big, all around, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there people guarding it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were. Yeah, there was-- you cannot-- when I went out, I crawled under. There were certain little things that you could go under the fence on your stomach. you just crawled out. You just-- I didn't go over the fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding it? Was it Germans or Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was Jewish. German was-- there were Germans, there was the Ukrainian police. But the ghetto I think was all the Jewish police and Jewish-- this was like a little, you know, a little government, but by itself. But-- under influence-- influence from the German. Whatever they said that had to be done. It wasn't just like, you know, a government. Whatever was-- what they-- the German told, there was like a Gemeinde, you know, like what is an office. And they-- they told the office what to do, come to send away today, who goes tomorrow, to these, to places to work, and things like that. We had to go out to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was like a Judenrat, or like the government of Jews that made the decisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yeah, the decisions-- the decisions, the Germans tell them what to do. And they made the decisions, like, you know, the person and the job. Even I went, as a little-- a young kid. And even younger kids than I, when they were making a-- building a street from, you know, like cable-- cable-- cable stones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cobblestones.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cobblestones you call it. The people had to prepare, had to dig out it, you know, make-- prepare the-- the street to be able to put the cobblestones or so. Also the bridge, or anything that had to be cleaned in this-- in town. They took only the ghetto people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were these Jewish policemen, were they kind to the Jewish people, or were they mean? Did you find that they were pretty fair?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know very little of it. I don't-- I really can't tell you much. I was-- I wasn't interested in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother? I don't think she worked. She-- maybe she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or any of your siblings, your brothers or sisters, have to-- well, you didn't know what your brother had to do. But did your sisters have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother wasn't in the-- with the men's side yet, because he was too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did-- did he have to work, or did any of your sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anybody, anybody who could work, and two-- had two hands. Babies didn't work. But others, like four years old, five years old, they send them to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same, whatever they told me to do. We were groups. They took us always in groups, not like just one person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was most of your work in the ghetto, or did you ever work outside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was done outside the ghetto. In the ghetto [LAUGHS] there was-- that was really outside the ghetto, when they took us to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it far away? Was is in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- the whole town is not-- wasn't big. So streets, like a street away from the ghetto, two streets, some center of the-- of the-- of the city, or the town rather, that they needed it. That was they send us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember at all, or could you figure out, was the work that they gave you, was it real work that had to be done, or did they just make hard work to torture the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I assume it was supposed-- it had to be done. They wanted it. Because we-- in a small town, we really had-- didn't have any-- we had streets, but they were not, not paved or anything. So they wanted to improve. They wanted-- they wanted-- you know, the Germans, they thought they're probably going to be there forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so they wanted to improve the town for the non-Jewish people, and the Jewish people did the work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, the Jewish people didn't-- were not in the-- in consideration.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were just used as machines?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a curfew that you couldn't go out past a certain time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was like, 8 o'clock or so curfew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did people stick to the curfew?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if they didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, I really don't know. Everybody was scared. I assume everybody did what they-- what we was told to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any-- was there a semblance of a regular life there? Did people try to have schooling or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No school. This was no, no. No school.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So on a day that you didn't go to work, what was your routine like? You and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my parents, with my mother and with the children outside in the ghetto. And in the ghetto, we did-- what like what kids are doing, play, drink, walk around, talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- was there regular prayers held at all at any place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Shabbos observed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't keep-- your family-- like the people in the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean here was the problem. We didn't go to-- to shul. The shul was there, but nobody went. It wasn't used as a shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, but did you keep Shabbos in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I assume so. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember. And holidays, Pesach or Sukkot, do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think we came to the holidays. When they were-- we were-- we were in the ghetto for maybe nine months or so. We went into the ghetto-- it was the beginning of the summer, I think. And-- and-- and the ghetto finished was like in September. We stayed a very-- a very short time in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, did you have to worry about, like cold weather and staying warm? Did you have any of those problems? How was the weather there by the time you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when-- it was cool already when-- as a matter of fact, when I took-- well, I will get to it, I guess at a--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Say what you want to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Say what you want I say.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when I jump from the attic, I had my mother's winter jacket on. I don't know how I put it on me. Don't ask me how come I had it on me. But I did. And that's saved-- that's how I lived through the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You're right. We'll get back to that. When it got cold at night, was there-- was there provisions for heat? Did people have coal or wood or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was-- we slept all-- the whole family slept together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that kept you warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Mother and the children, all together. We were sleeping in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said before you took clothes with you. Did you also take winter clothes, or just what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever we had, the winter clothes. We didn't have much clothes to begin with. Whatever it was. That's why my mother had the jacket. It was-- her-- her coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever-- do you remember the names of any of the people who were in charge, the Jewish guards, do you remember anybody's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the guard-- the main person their name was Wachs-- Wachs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wachs, could you-- could you figure out how to spell that? Is that like W-A--

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-S-- Wachs, or a G?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or W-A-C-H-S?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Wachs.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was he a young man, an older man? Do you remember about what age he was, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think he was middle-- middle age, maybe [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if people were appointed by the Nazis to be in the-- in the police-- in the Jewish police force?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose so. But I-- to tell you the truth, I really don't know. I think it's-- that's the way it was. But if it's-- I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know if people volunteered or if they were appointed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have German soldiers going through the ghetto at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the time when they liquidated the ghetto, I think they were there. But otherwise, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was nothing like women being raped or-- or disturbed in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. That was-- that was after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, you said that you escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the rumors were going around that they will-- that it-- what happened in other towns, cities that they take people and they-- they take them from the ghetto, and they bring-- take them to the-- to massive graves. As a matter of fact, my-- my father, the day they took from our ghetto the men to Kostopol. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we do that together. K-O-S-T-O-P-O-L.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they-- they dig the graves for other-- for people from other areas. Then when-- after a while, they sent the group, which my father was in, back to the ghetto. And they took another group of men, and send to Kostopol. Then the other group came back after a few weeks, and my father's group went back. And that was the last time I saw my father. And this went-- this is where when they dig the graves for us, for the Stepan ghetto, and all around, a little. I never saw the grave, because I ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, when-- when it started, when it came to our-- our-- to liquidate our ghetto, the night before I worked as a-- I was babysitting for a Ukrainian family at that time. Outside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside the ghetto, yeah. This went through the-- legally-- I did not sneak out to do that. And the-- they came that day in the afternoon. They came to us to-- to ask for horse and buggy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Horse and a buggy?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] yeah. And my-- the family that I babysit for, they had-- they were farmers. And they worked on the farm, and not too far from the house. So I said, no. I don't know anything. And that was Ukrainian people. And I don't know anything, but the people on the farm. So I guess they went there, and they told them for why they need it. And then they came back, and they send me home. And then-- and that was the end of it. I never went back. I mean that-- that was the night when they came, and they-- that-- that liquidating-- they liquidated our ghetto. So again, we in the house, we-- me and my-- and my-- and my mother and my sisters and brothers, and other people, the people that lived in that house decided to go up in the attic, and take the-- we had like a-- to going up to the attic, we had a-- a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A ladder?

speaker: interviewee
text: A ladder. And we took the ladder with us, so in case they're gonna-- they're gonna-- gonna come up, they'll see we don't-- that nobody is there. But-- and also we knew from others in other towns that if-- let's say it happened one night, and whoever survived that night-- they-- they didn't bother anymore. They lived another lot-- not-- a little bit longer. And then they did it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why we did this. We went all-- all, the whole house, the people in the house, went up-- in the attic. And we were laying all through the night, hoping that the morning will come, and they will-- they will stop. They'll be finished, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They'll be finished. Meanwhile, this really didn't happen in our town, in my town. They-- they-- because of-- in the morning, we were-- we heard the gestapo speaking. They spoke in-- in German. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: That means, Jews out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews, yeah. Because if you're not going to go-- come out, we will-- we'll find you. We'll shoot you on the spot. And they were-- there was shooting going around all through there, crying, and-- and killing, and shooting. And we was just stuck up there in the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally, they came to our house. They opened the door. And they says, any Juden? Any Juden? Is there any Jewish people there? And we didn't say nothing. We said nothing. Then they said, I know. Where's-- where's the ladder? Where's the ladder from the-- from to go up there? I know you must be there. So finally we look down, and that was in the barn, that was like a-- and we looked up-- down. And there we saw the gestapo, two guys, I saw. I remember the brown uniforms. And maybe there was more. And we started-- and I and my mother stood right there, looking down. And we started-- they started to put-- to move to lower the-- the ladder down. Who started to lower the ladder?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people from up-- from the-- from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for them to come up, or to us to come down rather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Why did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted us down. They wanted to kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but who put the ladder-- you said the people that were upstairs with you were lowering the ladder.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to. If not, they would come up themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: They knew that you were there? They knew that were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, they said they knew that the ladder is up. The people must be there. So they said, if you're not gonna do that, we're going to climb up. We'll find you anyway. So they started-- I don't know even remember who started to move the ladder down. And I stood next to my mother, and in a spare moment, I-- something came into my mind. And I left my mother, and I jumped to the other side of the house. There was the [? sukkah. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I jumped, and after me jumped another-- the young girl, at that time a young-- year younger than I was, and my mother's cousin, a woman maybe in her early 30s. Do you remember the names of the people that you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah. The one is Perl, and the other one is Sassel. But she never-- she didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Perl and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me, no. No. And then we came out to the-- outside in the street. And we saw nobody there. And we decided to go to climb over the fence, and run-- and to run away. And that's exactly what we did. I did. I started to go. I was the first one. We got out. Then that was Ukraine-- the Ukrainian side. Naturally the Ukrainian, there was children and adults watching what's going on in the ghetto. They started to chase us, the-- the kids. And they started to yell, there-- there comes the gestapo. And make arrest, police, Jewish people, Jewish kids running! And we kept running, and we kept on running. And we decided we'll go to the cemetery. So we went to the Jewish cemetery. And when we got there, the same thing was there happening. Everybody was-- there's crying and shooting was going on. So we decided not to go into the cemetery, and go-- we were going into further into the fields, to the farm-- where the farmers lived. And finally it started-- we find-- there at that time, they were cut-- they were-- that was the time when they were cutting the corn and the wheat, and they put such-- they made such a things heaped, you know, for this to dry. And we went into this. And each one was like separately. And we-- and then we went into a barn. And then some-- during the night, I hear people walking and yelling, Ukrainian, the police. We police, if there's anybody there, out. Otherwise, we'll kill you. So we just-- we-- the three of us, we-- we were crawling on our stomach out from the barn. And we went further into the-- again, into the fields. And for some reason we were lucky, they didn't see us. They didn't hear anything. And then day came, daytime came. And we got up, out of [INAUDIBLE], and we started to walk to look for a place where there's no Ukrainian people, where the Polish people live. And this-- and then they met two more, two other kids, boys, who-- who-- and we teamed up. And we all were walking together, just walking in the middle of the streets, didn't know who-- the people didn't know us. The kids didn't know us. They didn't say-- we just walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then we-- until we reached the Polish, Wirke, that town.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you-- did you feel that the Poles would be kinder to you than the Ukrainians? Is that why you went here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukrainians, you were afraid were going to tell on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I believe, if not for-- if the Ukrainians wouldn't team up with the Germans, more people would survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Ukrainians were-- [BACKROUND NOISE] were telling on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- yeah, the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the two boys that you teamed up with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah one is Mikhal, and he's in Israel, and has a beautiful family. And the other one is Lazer. Lazer-- that's-- or Lazer, whatever. He's-- I never saw him. But he was, I think, Lazer I believe is in Canada somewhere. But that's what Mikhal told me. But Perl is in-- is in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perl, that's the girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perl, my friend-- the girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that like 'Pearl'?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perl, like Perl. You know, we call Perl. She lives-- she was-- she's married, has two children. Her husband died by now. And Mikhal is there. Mikhal was my best friend, his family, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what's his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time it was-- it was Pieczenik.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now-- can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pieczenik is like-- no. I can write it down, if you want it from me. We have everything in writing. We didn't spell in Poland. You want me to-- do you want see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pieczenik?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pieczenik? Oh, it doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other boy? And that was Mikhal and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Lazer, Lazer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now his name is, I think his name is altogether different. It's not Pieczenik.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it an Israeli name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he picked his Israeli name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. I forgot at this minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. If you think of it, you just say it. And Lazer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Stepan? No, no. See with Pieczenik, we knew better than the other family. We all knew his parents too. Everybody knew each other, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so the people that you met when you were fleeing were people from Stepan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh yeah, yeah. That's for sure. Then rumors-- what when we finally-- when we started to hide-- and the-- we were hiding in this forest. And people-- people that lived close to the forest, they-- we used to go to ask for food. And every day we would move to a different place. And this lady happened to be-- that she couldn't be with us children. Because she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: The lady, my cousin's-- I said that my mother's cousin who jumped. She went daylight, in daylight, to beg for food. And they were-- and they corrupted her, and that killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a woman, she wasn't a child like you? She was older?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was in her early 30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now you said before that in the ghetto, you heard rumors. Do you know how-- how the rumors were brought over to you? What-- like what was the source of your information?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just heard people talking about things? And was it usually correct what they talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so now-- you weren't very-- were you far away from Stepan by now, what you're telling us? Or were you still pretty close to Stepan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- I don't know if that's about-- it was kilometers-- I don't know how. But it was a little far. Yeah. We walked all day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see people from other ghettos that were escaping?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met four kids in-- in Wirke. We were hiding in the-- a part of in the forest that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of it? What did you say, Wirke?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wirke, the town of the-- that was the town that we-- actually the forest that we--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's W-I-R-K-E?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we met them, and they wanted to-- we and [INAUDIBLE], and we were all like teaming up together. And then rumors were going around in town, in Wirke, that there are four Jewish kids hiding. And they were after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But somehow, by moving from every day to different areas, they couldn't-- they didn't caught up with us. But that day, when we met that-- the four other boys from a difference town, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were sitting together. And I said to them, you know what? That's not a good idea to sit together. Because I know they know there-- there's four kids. Where were you sitting? Where was this? Where were you located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest. Yeah. And so they separated, and we all-- like not too far. But they caught them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They caught--

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the other boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All of a sudden we hear walking steps, you know. And it was-- you-- in the forest you hear, if somebody walks, you hear walking. And we took a peek, like behind the-- in between the-- the bushes. And I saw-- I saw the four boys and the policeman walking with them, after them with a gun, with a-- with a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bayonet?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, what is a big-- the rifle, the rifle. And they killed them. They were gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see them kill them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. And then we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names? Did you ever get to know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What season was this? Was it cold already?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was like September time. To me, it's-- I think it was September, maybe it's beginning of October. But it's something like this, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So It wasn't very cold yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because right after, winter came. And we had-- we had-- we couldn't sleep in-- in the forest. So this Polish lady gave us-- let us into the house once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same-- you went-- you went back to the same Polish lady all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all the time, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean when you went back sometimes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And some others did too. Some others did too, let us in, or they lets us sleep in the barn or so. Because it was very dangerous for them to let us in. Because they knew if they-- if anybody was hiding Jewish people, they'll be-- they'll kill them, just the same as the Jewish. So they were really--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, were these people that you knew from before, or they were strangers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strangers, completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get to know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well my Polish family, who survived it, is-- is-- was Janicki.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a hard one. You know, we'll do a-- we'll write it at the break, and then we'll show it later on. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I have the--

speaker: interviewer
text: In fact, we're going to take the break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll be back with another tape.
